>rc buys 7,780,000 shares of BBBY, and $60-$80 strike calls worth 1,670,100 shares in March 2022

>rc sells shares and calls of BBBY in august 2022

>rc has a sitdown with a famous corporate raider, Carl Icahn in october 2022

>rc offers 400 million for BBBY in December 2022

>pitchbook (best M&A software suite in the world, costing 5 figures + per year) lists BBBY(now known as BBBYQ or DK-Butterfly-1) as sold via a leveraged buyout on January 13 2023

>a default on the ABL with JPM happened on the same day that pitchbook shows a leveraged buyout, the terms of that ABL specify that a default is triggered if a change of control happens, a change of control is defined as an acquisition of 40% or more of the voting power by any person or group.

>one month later, February 2023 RC tweets “someone tell the government to stop shooting down my balloons”, alluding to bad actors delaying the Bed Bath m&a

>the GameStop CEO then became a BBBYQ chapter 11 creditor and debtor

>Larry cheng (who helped RC build Chewy, and is a current boardmember at GameStop, and a close personal friend to RC) went onto theppshow podcast (which is dedicated to BBBY recovery theories) 8 months after BBBY filed for chapter 11, and 2 months after shares were cancelled

>OverStock - who is the most famous company to battle illegal naked shorts, who even released a crypto dividend 5 years ago - purchased the BBBY IP, and owns 55% of TZero - the only approved blockchain entity who can issue debt instruments and securities

>OverStock - whos now traded under ticker BBBY - has issued warrants with dates that match GME's warrants, and with a CUSIP-6 that matches BBBYQ's CUSIP-6, proving BBBYQ has already begun m&a activity with BBBY


REQUIRED READING: bbbwhy.com

>Larry Cheng and Bill Pulte have gone onto theppshow podcast

item.title: Post-work society — 12 replies — 48.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Post-work society

Open thread • Replies: 12 • Updated: 2026-07-04 15:17 UTC

OP

Am I correct in predicting that in the future only a few people will have a traditional job?

There's a guy who makes My Little Pony fleshlights out of silicone and sells them at 500 dollars a pop.
A lot of guys stream on Kick and make more than doctors.
Millions of young women shake their ass on Tiktok and Chaturbate, again making more than doctors.
The list goes on.
I'm starting to see a phenomenon where people no longer go to school, they all find some online niche to fill and then get rich.

Is this what everyone will be doing in 2050? Become an entertainer or die?
I've always hated the right-wing attitude that everyone has to make their own money. I want UBI, not this shit.

Early replies (first 3)

62445033
>the pedo elite won't give us UBI
>instead they give us platforms like Onlyfans where we humiliate ourselves for the right to continue existing

They're making fun of us.

62445042
We don't know how to transition to a world where people access shared wealth. It's a very difficult coordination problem because everyone wants as much wealth as possible. So we're starting to use labor as an artificial need signal. I.e. if you need the wealth really badly, jump through these hoops, and you will prove yourself deserving of that wealth. Even the Soviets forced people to make surplus steel because they didn't know how to just give wealth without using labor as a filter. Unfortunately, this means that in the limit 99% of labor will be pretty much useless.

62445052
>>62445042

But the system isn't meritocratic. I see people far less deserving than me making a thousand times what I make.
I've spent 10 years studying, learning skills, applying for jobs etc. and I'm still in square one. Then there are IQ 60 schizophrenics who make an account on Kick and people watch them because they're entertaining and crazy.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62445065
>>62445052
So your jealous ,,,,sucks to be you i guess there's always gonna be someone with more than you

62445103
>>62445052
No it is, you simply can't learn the skills that that low iq has, so you are not deserving. You aren't as all round as you make yourself out to be.

62445261
>>62445031
all the shit you listed is the byproduct of idiots having too much spare money
one good market correction will end all of these with a vengeance

62445375
>>62445031
UBI is just a pipedream. The math doesn't work. And the social consequences are devastating. If you give people large handouts they turn to drugs and rot. Really small amounts, like $500 a month or something are a little more mixed, but that's effectively what you have with current welfare programs and also not what you're describing. Outside of experiments you have natural observations of what happens when people receive guaranteed income in different amounts by looking at Indian reservations with casinos of differing sizes. The most successful have tribe members guaranteed six figures a year for life, and the results are still mostly a bunch of layabout alcoholics, though they can afford medical treatments and stuff.

>I'm starting to see a phenomenon where people no longer go to school, they all find some online niche to fill and then get rich

If you look at rich people a lot of them will have done this. But of people not going to school very few will be able to get rich doing this. It will be more common for talented people to ignore college, but this is for a single, sub single digit percentage.

62445416
>>62445033
it's the equivalent of all these scifi dystopian stories where they go do games and contests to get out of the rat race. but might just be worse because not even in those stories are they doing weird degrading sexual humiliation jobs

62445425
>>62445065
gay id spotted

62445434
civilizations across the centuries have had many periods in time in which the masses became too indulgent, hedonistic, with salivating base desires. the issue is that the times in which there is a balance do not last as long as when times are overly strict/repressive and overly indulgent/hedonistic. Both extremes lead to inequality of disproportionate amount in which the humans at their breaking point eventually demand change, swinging the pendulum again. will humans ever be able to achieve balance for longer periods of time? or will the monkey brain always win out no matter what? we need to rid ourselves of the monkey within.

62445439
>>62445031
>A lot of guys stream on Kick and make more than doctors.

Yeah no

62445443
>>62445261
True

item.title: Why are younger people encouraged to make greater risks when it comes to investing? — 14 replies — 41.3/hr
item.content:encoded:
Why are younger people encouraged to make greater risks when it comes to investing?

Open thread • Replies: 14 • Updated: 2026-07-04 14:57 UTC

OP

Why are younger people encouraged to make greater risks when it comes to investing? Doesn't it make much more sense to invest in a low risk ETF while you're younger and have more time? Why should some 22 year old who just learned about the stock market yesterday put all their money into what's essentially gambling as opposed to VOO or some shit that's bound to go up?

Early replies (first 3)

62444847
>bro just invest for the next 50 years so u can and a golden nest egg at 70 years old maybe

Look at the demographics

Look at the browning of the population

Imagine thinking the last 50 years of growth is gonna happen again in the next 50 years

You should be doing everything in your power to make it NOW, not LATER

62444848
>>62444837
because they've got 50 years of waging ahead of them, so greater risk for greater reward makes sense, given they will be able to get through any hard times unscathed due to aforementioned bulk years of earning potential remainig. low risk investing is for old cunts who have limited earning potential remaining, or have retired already.

62444851
>>62444837
Speaking as someone who helped advertise crypto in the second btc bullcycle, younger people are easier marks. They tend to be idealistic and there's a lot of dumb money among trust fund kids, techbros, people who did well with crypto once and thing they have a magic touch, etc. Old people are nervous.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62444856
>>62444837
i always tell young people that there is no point in saving money because AI+Robots will be able to do everything, resulting in universal high income. Work will be optional.

62444860
>>62444837
Two reasons:
First, they have less to lose, so it's the best time to learn and look for higher upside. Financial losses are not as devastating or impactful when they are just starting to invest as opposed to when they're closer to retirement.
Second, young people have a lot less time to make it nowadays than old people, what with the demographic collapse, gerintocracy with exponentially increasing pension and health spending, etc. Young people are taxed more, make less and the gap will only widen as the largest generation retires.

62444869
>>62444860
OP unironically thinks everything is gonna play out the same as it did for him as it did for boomers kek
DO YOU FAGGOTS NOT UNDERSTAND

THE NONS ARE TAKING OVER

THEY WILL GAIN ALL THE POLITICAL POWER SOON

THEY WILL ABSOLUTELY RAID THE FUCK OUT OF ALL YOUR WHITE BOY RETIREMENT FUNDS


I can understand making sure you have something later in life, but for the love of GOD, do NOT save within the system that HATES YOU, because you will eventually get it all taken from you, by the NON's

At least have it in something that they can't simply take from you, like Bitcoin, Monero, Ethereum, Solana, etc, whatever the fuck

62444873
>>62444837
When you're young you have very little to lose, and a lot of time to regain losses. When you're old, it's the opposite.

Not really a hard concept to wrap your head around

62444884
>>62444873
>>62444873
>When you're young you have very little to lose, and a lot of time to regain losses. When you're old, it's the opposite.

Even this is retarded logic, because if youre old, it means youre near death anyway, so wtf do you care about "having a safety net" when youre almost dead?


My grandfather had a multi-million dollar home, and he still ended up needing full-time care in a nursing home for the last few years of his life

Even if he was a rentoid, he would have still ended up in the same nursing home.

62444912
>>62444884
You are retarded but think you know everything

62444916
>>62444884
>when youre almost dead?
almost dead yeah, who gives a fuck. The 15-20 years of retirement before dropping dead is where having a 'safety net' is probably a wise move.

62444952
>>62444916
I know a guy who made it, saved, invested, died from cancer in his 60s, didn't even get to enjoy retirement.

I know another guy, same age as him, who did not invest, has no assets, and lives on a government pension, and is alive and healthy, but isnt well off, he just lives from gov payment to gov payment

Do you see how life works now? Being a good boy and safely investing wont save you from the side effects of old age.

Live your life and go fucking crazy with high risk shit in the hope you make it when you're still young enough to enjoy it.

62445278
>>62444837
Paying chain smoking coke-heads to gamble for you. Including short selling which can go horribly wrong. There are other ways to invest via broker that does not short sell stocks. Also learning how to do it themselves can reap big rewards. Lots of people were blocked by their brokers from investing in hydrograph. Even tiny amounts. Same thing with Game Stop. Took enough retail being interested to change their stances on the first one. The 2nd one was too much of a high risk gamble that they didn't want to be liable for. Especially when it took a whole new experimental investment strategy.

Sometimes it takes retail to get the ball rolling before the big investors notice that it is a good investment they overlooked. But if everyone is in ETFs then new innovations or products can be easily killed in the cradle or sent to an early grave before it fully catches on.

>>62444869
>>62444873
This. Boomers already got screwed twice in the 2000's. The favored pampered demographic they did everything for. If they were worse to you for everything else what makes you think they won't be worse when it comes to protecting your digital investments? Do not give them full autonomy over what stocks are bought and sold and when. They might make the decision for you to be a bag holder on purpose without your consent.

62445291
>>62445278
Paragraphs of nonsense because you dont understand business

62445387
>>62444847
You forget the quintessential rule: nothing ever happens.

Buffett gave his kids $90k (roughly $600-700k in today's money) in Berkshire stock when they turned 19.
Peter started a record business, Howard started an excavator business, and I can't remember what Susie did (something involving art galleries), but they all squandered it, with barely a second thought, which is shocking given how much Buffett harps on
>buy and hold
Clearly, he didn't instill that virtue in his own children.
He helped them somewhat in others ways: boguht a farm for Howard, but charged him rent.
He also notoriously would bribe them to not be fat, subjecting them to weighings, as well as offering them rewards if he failed to maintain a given weight. (Not sure if this is based or psychotic.)

62444184
>>62444170
>He helped them somewhat in others ways: boguht a farm for Howard, but charged him rent.
>He also notoriously would bribe them to not be fat, subjecting them to weighings, as well as offering them rewards if he failed to maintain a given weight. (Not sure if this is based or psychotic.)
in the case of the farm, btw, the rent was based in part on how much Howard weighed.
My point was that Buffett actually did give a great deal of assistance to his kids, even if he was a skinflint in other respects.
And his kids mostly squandered it.

62444231
>>62443887
why would i pay for golf clubs and polos and fancy watches and a WASP-y country club lifestyle when thats not what i personally want?

after a decade of being on my own, i just want to retire as soon as possible and spend as little as possible. even if it means being a single hermit, i dont really care. i was on my own, so i had to determine what was best for me. now, at the eleventh hour, i am being accosted for "being weird" because what i want and what work towards isnt what my dad expected i would do, basically. again, im not complaining. im just raising the questionn, how does one delicately explain that when you give zero suport, you take yourself away from influencing outcomes.

62444276
>>62442989
to be totally honest i see my father as the one was wronged, by the bad parenting advice that comes from financial talking heads like ramsey and buffet who insist to boomers they need to cut off support to their kids at 18. my dad could have had a son with the lifestyle he wanted to see his son have. he just thought it would magically happen with no financial or job finding help. i actually feel very sorrowful with respect to the situation, i think it was a perfectly avoidable, unoptimal outcome for everyone involved.
i dont see myself as a victim at all, i have a very fortunate life in many ways.

62444331
>>62443265
i dont think my dad ever wanted to necessarily give regal commands from afar, he just assumed- i believe- that despite having a son who was an indebted college kid totally on their own, he thought i was going to gravitate towards the same life path he followed and all his friends kids followed. but after being thrown in the deep end of life, i decided i wanted to retire ASAP even if it means living like an impoverished NEET for 10 years, rather than retire at 65 after decades of living with an extravagent country club WASP-y lifestyle. i dont think the possibility entered his mind that someone could consider "success" in life as something different than boater shoes and pickleball and golfing at the country club. and to be clear, i would have been happy to be a Brayden with a 2.1 GPA in business and a job set up for me. but im also happy being a pseudo NEET goblin emulating Phantasy Star Online for gamecube and daytrading my salary this week, instead of spending a few hundred dollars golfing. honestly its whatever.

62444339
This thread only only shows how demoralization won on both sides of the coin. There is an outside enemy that want you destroyed and nothing less, not that you don’t deserve it with the way things have regressed.

62444345
>>62444276
>and buffet

>>62444184
>>62444191
Buffett did not "cut off" his kids at 18. He gave them each $90k ($500-600k adjusted for inflation) in BRK stock on their 19th birthday, in addition to paying their college, and other stuff (but the "other stuff" usually had strings attached).
Howard Buffett's BRK b-day gift would be worth $236,262,411 today if he never sold any shares.

Personally I actually would have loved to have started a family by now, but when you're thrown into the deep end of life and explicitly told- in no uncertain terms- no life raft will be provided to you... At least for me, that totally evaporated any and all romantic or family-starting instincts within me, for a long time. My only priority for years was finding stability and securing solid income and investments. After all, I personally watched multiple friends in college flunk out of our degree program after becoming depressed after a bad break up. Why would I risk dating when a bad breakup could end up in me throwing away my GPA, and internship/job prospects, as a potential result?

Now that I have stable income and a solid portfolio, now that I have breathing room, it's uncomfortably apparent how unnecessary the whole ordeal was and how much happier everyone in the situation would be, if boomers weren't convinced en masse that an adult kid asking for money in college was, in every case, a parasitic leech.

>>62442253
The “Gish Gallop” and its prepositional permutations—Gish for Gallop, Gish of Gallop, Gish on Gallop, etc.—mark distinct ontological orientations of rhetorical velocity. To retort technically, one must treat the Gallop not as of Gish but as through Gish: a phenomenon where assertional acceleration surpasses epistemic friction. The Gallop is discourse as kinematic excess—an epistemological overclocking.
Gish for Gallop implies teleology: rhetoric mobilized toward perpetual propulsion. Gish of Gallop encodes possession—the Gallop as Gish’s offspring. Gish on Gallop situates argument atop momentum, while Gish in Gallop dissolves distinction, embedding Gish within motion itself. Gish at Gallop denotes encounter, the cognition of discursive overwhelm. Gish from Gallop reverses causality, rendering the effect its own origin.
Gish about Gallop becomes reflexive critique—commentary risking absorption by its object. Gish under and below Gallop expose the substructure: unspoken premises enabling the deluge. Gish above Gallop implies transcendence, the meta-discursive gaze seeking order in chaos. Gish near and around Gallop orbit the phenomenon, maintaining analytical proximity without dissolution.
Most fertile is Gish between Gallop—a liminal field where assertion and absence interlace, revealing discourse as interval rather than totality. Finally, Gish Gallop stands bare, tautological and absolute: persuasion through proliferation, logos devolved into kinetics. Each form rearticulates the same epistemic entropy—truth displaced by momentum, meaning fragmented by excess. The Gish Gallop, thus, is not fallacy but cosmology: rhetoric as perpetual acceleration toward the vanishing point of comprehension.
